summary:
Evisceration involves removing all contents within a damaged eye socket; it’s usually reserved only when other options fail with conditions causing significant intraocular complications. Expulsive hemorhages are often treated less radically making this option unlikely requiring full evisceration procedures among listed choices. Agent2 concluded that 'expulsive hemorrhage' was most appropriate answer since usual treatment methods tend towards minimally invasive interventions instead of complete ocular content excision under normal medical practice guidelines provided no specific reference explicitly stated otherwise regarding these scenarios. Therefore:

Evisceration should primarily address major threats inside eyes leading toward functional loss prevention whereas minor issues get handled differently thus ruling out A,B,C leaving us at choice D which aligns best based described context clues about standard care protocols used today by ophthalmologists globally concerning emergency cases involving eyeballs integrity preservation versus total extraction strategies employed solely during extreme situations outlined here.
